# Express Validator via Standard Schema Interface (ArkType)

## Background

ArkType implements the https://standardschema.dev specification: every schema exposes a `~standard` property with a `validate(value)` method that returns either a typed success result or a list of issues. Build a small Express service that performs ALL request validation through this vendor-neutral interface — not through ArkType's own `.assert()` / direct-invocation APIs.

## Requirements

* Implement an Express HTTP service in `/home/user/myproject` listening on port 3000.
* Define two ArkType schemas (using `arktype@2.2.0`):
  * A body schema for `POST /users` with `username` (alphanumeric, length 3..20), `email` (valid email), and an optional `age` (integer in \[13, 120]).
  * A query schema for `GET /search` with `q` (string, length 1..100), `page` (integer >= 1), and `limit` (integer in \[1, 50]). Because Express query values arrive as strings, the schema MUST coerce strings to numbers using ArkType morphs (a single declarative pipeline — no `JSON.parse`, `parseInt`, or other hand-written coercion).
* Implement a reusable middleware factory `validate(source, schema)` where `source` is either `'body'` or `'query'`. It MUST:
  * Drive validation exclusively through the Standard Schema interface (i.e. call the schema's `~standard.validate(...)` and inspect its `value` / `issues`).
  * On success: replace `req[source]` with the validated/coerced value and call `next()`.
  * On failure: respond with HTTP 400 and a JSON body containing an `issues` array.
* Wire both routes through `validate(...)`. On success:
  * `POST /users` responds `201` with a JSON body echoing the validated user.
  * `GET /search` responds `200` with a JSON body echoing the validated/coerced query (numeric `page` and `limit`).

## Implementation Hints

* Read the Standard Schema spec at https://standardschema.dev to understand the exact shape of `~standard.validate` and its result type.
* ArkType morphs (the `|>` / pipe operator) can be used to coerce a string into a number before applying further numeric constraints; consult https://arktype.io/docs/expressions for available expressions.
* The middleware must remain schema-agnostic: it should work with any object that satisfies the Standard Schema interface, regardless of which library produced it.
* Treat the schema's `validate` result as potentially asynchronous (it may return a Promise).

## Acceptance Criteria

* Project path: /home/user/myproject
* Start command: `npx tsx server.ts`
* Port: 3000
* API Endpoints:
  * `POST /users`
    ```json
    // Request body
    { "username": string, "email": string, "age": number /* optional */ }
    ```
    ```json
    // 201 Response
    { "username": string, "email": string, "age": number /* if provided */ }
    ```
    On validation failure: status `400` with body `{ "issues": [ { "message": string, ... } , ... ] }`.
  * `GET /search?q=...&page=...&limit=...`
    ```json
    // 200 Response
    { "q": string, "page": number, "limit": number }
    ```
    `page` and `limit` MUST be numbers in the response (coerced from the incoming string query values).
    On validation failure: status `400` with body `{ "issues": [ ... ] }`.
* The middleware factory MUST be defined in source and its implementation MUST reference the Standard Schema property literally (the string `~standard` must appear in the middleware source).
* `arktype@2.2.0`, `express`, and `tsx` are preinstalled in `/home/user/myproject`.
I'll start by examining the project directory and understanding what's already set up.
Now let me read the existing project files and examine ArkType's API for the Standard Schema interface and morph expressions.
Now let me check how ArkType exposes the `~standard` property and look at the Standard Schema implementation in ArkType:
Now let me check how ArkType's `Type` class exposes `~standard` and how morphs work:

Title: ArkType Docs

URL Source: https://arktype.io/docs/expressions

Published Time: Wed, 04 Mar 2026 20:23:49 GMT

Markdown Content:
### [Intersection](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#intersection)

Like its TypeScript counterpart, an intersection combines two existing `Type`s to create a new `Type` that enforces the constraints of both.

If you don't need to intersect overlapping props, spread instead!

Computing an intersection is more expensive than [merging props](https://arktype.io/docs/objects#merge), both at runtime and in-editor.

If you can get away with the latter, doing so will make your Types faster and cleaner.

#### [n-ary](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#n-ary)

To create an intersection of many objects directly, `type.and` will avoid the need to chain or compose many binary expressions:

### [Union](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#union)

All unions are automatically discriminated to optimize check time and error message clarity.

#### [n-ary](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#n-ary-1)

To create a union of many objects directly, `type.or` will avoid the need to chain or compose many binary expressions:

A union that could apply different morphs to the same data throws a ParseError!

Learn the set theory behind this restriction
If you're relatively new to set-based types, that error might be daunting, but if you take a second to think through the example, it becomes clear why this isn't allowed. The logic of `bad` is essentially:

*   If the input is an object where `box` is a `string`, parse and return it as a number
*   If the input is an object where `box` is a `string`, return it as a string

There is no way to deterministically return an output for this type without sacrificing the [commutativity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Commutative_property) of the union operator.

`sameError` may look more innocuous, but has the same problem for an input like `{ a: "1", b: "2" }`.

*   Left branch would only parse `a`, resulting in `{ a: 1, b: "2" }`
*   Right branch would only parse `b`, resulting in `{ a: "1", b: 2 }`

### [Brand](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#brand)

Add a type-only symbol to an existing type so that the only values that satisfy it are those that have been directly validated.

Brands can be a great way to represent constraints that fall outside the scope TypeScript, but remember they don't change anything about what is enforced at runtime!

For more information on branding in general, check out [this excellent article](https://www.learningtypescript.com/articles/branded-types) from [Josh Goldberg](https://github.com/joshuakgoldberg).

### [Narrow](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#narrow)

Narrow expressions allow you to add custom validation logic and error messages. You can read more about them in [their intro section](https://arktype.io/docs/intro/adding-constraints#narrow).

If the return type of a narrow is a type predicate, that will be reflected in the inferred `Type`.

### [Filter](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#filter)

`filter` is similar to `narrow` but applies its predicate to the **input** of a type rather than the output. This distinction matters when your type includes morphs:

*   **`narrow`**: validates the **output** (after morphs have been applied)
*   **`filter`**: validates the **input** (before morphs run)

If the return type of a filter is a type predicate, that will be reflected in the inferred `Type`, just like with `narrow`.

### [Pipe](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#pipe)

Piping allows you to transform your data after it is validated via one or more sequential **morphs**. You can read more about them in [their intro section](https://arktype.io/docs/intro/morphs-and-more).

#### [To](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#to)

If a morph returns an `ArkErrors` instance, validation will fail with that result instead of it being treated as a value. This is especially useful for using other Types as morphs to validate output or chain transformations.

To make this easier, there's a special `to` operator that can pipe to a parsed definition without having to wrap it in `type` to make it a function:

#### [n-ary](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#n-ary-2)

Though the fluent `.pipe(...morphsOrTypes)` method already accepts any number of arguments, `type.pipe()` allows you to define such an expression directly without having to explicitly instantiate the first input Type to chain from.

### [Unit](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#unit)

While embedded [literal syntax](https://arktype.io/docs/primitives#number-literals) is usually ideal for defining exact primitive values, `===` and `type.unit` can be helpful for referencing a non-serializable value like a `symbol` from your type.

### [Enumerated](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#enumerated)

`type.enumerated` defines a Type based on a list of allowed values. It is semantically equivalent to `type.unit` if provided a single value.

### [valueOf](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#valueof)

`type.valueOf` defines a Type from a TypeScript `enum` or enum-like object.

`enum` should be avoided in modern TypeScript

Over time, TS has shifted away from features that affect the `.js` it ultimately outputs, including `enum`.

With the introduction of the [`--erasableSyntaxOnly` option](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/#erasableSyntaxOnly) to facilitate type-stripping, `enum` is no longer considered a best practice.

`type.valueOf` exists primarily to facilitate integration with legacy code that relies on `enum`, but if you have the option, prefer transparently defining value sets via `["tupleLiterals"] as const`, `{ objectLiterals: true } as const`, or directly via [`type.enumerated`](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#enumerated).

It is _almost_ semantically identical to `type.enumerated(...Object.values(o))`. The only exception occurs when an object has an entry with a numeric value and entry with that value as a key mapping back to the original:

Notice `EquivalentObject` doesn't include `"numeric"` because it inverts a numeric value entry.

We recommend `type.enumerated` as the more transparent option for converting value references to a Type. However, if the described inverted entry pairs can't exist on your object, you can safely use `type.valueOf`.

### [fn](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#fn)

`type.fn` defines a function with runtime-validated parameters and an optional return type. The result is a `TypedFn`- a callable with `.expression`, `.params`, and `.returns` for introspection.

Parameters are defined using the same syntax as tuple types. A return type can optionally be specified after a `":"` separator.

Supports defaults, optionals, and variadic parameters:

If no return type is specified, the return type is inferred from the implementation. If a return type is specified, it will be validated at runtime.

### [Meta](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#meta)

Metadata allows you to associate arbitrary metadata with your types.

Some metadata is consumed directly by ArkType, for example `description` is referenced by default when building an error message.

Other properties are introspectable, but aren't used by default internally.

For more control over how to apply the config to the Type's internal structure, a `NodeSelector` can be specified following the config:

For more details on the select API, [see the full docs](https://arktype.io/docs/introspection#select).

### [Cast](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#cast)

Sometimes, you may want to directly specify how a `Type` should be inferred without affecting the runtime behavior. In these cases, you can use a cast expression.

### [Parenthetical](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#parenthetical)

By default, ArkType's operators follow the same precedence as TypeScript's. Also like in TypeScript, this can be overridden by wrapping an expression in parentheses.

### [this](https://arktype.io/docs/expressions#this)

`this` is a special keyword that can be used to create a recursive type referencing the root of the current definition.

Referencing `this` from within a scope will result in a ParseError. For similar behavior within a scoped definition, just reference the alias by name:

part.text: Now let me check how ArkType implements the `~standard` property on its types, and look at morphs more closely:

Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me create the todo list and implement the solution.

Key findings:
1. ArkType's `Type` (which extends `BaseRoot`) has a `~standard` getter that returns `{ vendor, version, validate, jsonSchema }`.
2. The `validate` method calls the type as a function (`this(input)`) and returns either `{ value: out }` on success or the `ArkErrors` instance on failure.
3. `ArkErrors` has an `issues` getter that returns `this` (itself), and `ArkErrors` extends `ReadonlyArray<ArkError>` where each `ArkError` has a `message` property.
4. For morphs, ArkType uses the `|>` operator (pipe) to chain transformations. The string `"number"` parsed as a morph will coerce.

Let me now write the `server.ts`:

part.text:
The issue is that `"string |> number & number.integer >= 1"` is being parsed as `string |> (number & number.integer >= 1)` — the `&` binds tighter than `|>`. I need to use parentheses to group the pipe target. Let me check the precedence:

Looking at the operator parser, `|>` pushes the root to a branch, and `&` also pushes to a branch. The issue is that `|>` is a pipe operator and `&` is intersection, and the parser is trying to intersect `number` with `number.integer >= 1` before piping. I need to parenthesize the right side of the pipe.

Actually, looking more carefully, the issue is that `"string |> number & number.integer >= 1"` is being parsed as `string |> (number & number.integer >= 1)`, which tries to intersect `string` (the piped result) with `number` — but `string |> number` produces a morph type, and intersecting it with `number` would mean the output is `number & number.integer >= 1`. 

Wait, actually the error says "Intersection of string and number results in an unsatisfiable type". Let me re-read the parse. The `|>` creates a morph where the input is `string` and the output is `number`. Then `& number.integer >= 1` intersects the *output* with `number.integer >= 1`. But the `&` operator on a morph intersects the output type — that should work.

Hmm, let me look at this more carefully. The error says "Intersection of string and number" — so it seems like `&` is intersecting with the *input* (string) rather than the *output*. Let me try a different approach — use parentheses:

part.text:
Now I see. When we do `(string |> number) & number.integer >= 1`, the `&` operator is handled by `intersectNodesRoot`. Since `(string |> number)` has `includesTransform = true`, the intersection goes through `pipeMorphed`. In `_pipeMorphed`, since `from` (the morph `string |> number`) has `hasKind("morph")` and `from.lastMorphIfNode` is a node, it calls `intersectOrPipeNodes(from.lastMorphIfNode, to, ctx)` where `from.lastMorphIfNode` is the `number` type and `to` is `number.integer >= 1`. This should work — intersecting `number` with `number.integer >= 1` should give `number.integer >= 1`.

Wait, but the error says "Intersection of string and number". Let me look more carefully at what `from.lastMorphIfNode` is for `string |> number`. In this case, the morph's `lastMorph` is the `number` type itself (a `BaseRoot`). So `lastMorphIfNode` is `number`. Then `intersectOrPipeNodes(number, number.integer >= 1, ...)` should work fine.

Hmm, maybe the issue is that `from.inner.in` is being intersected instead. Let me trace through more carefully. Actually, looking at the error trace again:

```
at DomainNode.toNode (root.js:287:32)
at DomainNode.rawPipeOnce (root.js:292:25)
```

The `toNode` function is:
```js
toNode(root) {
    const result = pipeNodesRoot(this, root, this.$);
    if (result instanceof Disjoint)
        return result.throw();
    return result;
}
```

And `rawPipeOnce`:
```js
rawPipeOnce(morph) {
    if (hasArkKind(morph, "root"))
        return this.toNode(morph);
    ...
}
```

So the error is happening when `string |> number` is being constructed — `string.toNode(number)` calls `pipeNodesRoot(string, number, ...)`. Let me look at `_pipeNodes` again:

```js
const _pipeNodes = (l, r, ctx) => l.includesTransform || r.includesTransform ?
    ctx.invert ?
        pipeMorphed(r, l, ctx)
        : pipeMorphed(l, r, ctx)
    : _intersectNodes(l, r, ctx);
```

Neither `string` nor `number` has `includesTransform`, so it falls through to `_intersectNodes(l, r, ctx)`. `_intersectNodes` tries to find an intersection implementation for `string` and `number` — which doesn't exist (they're disjoint), so it returns `null`, and that likely becomes a Disjoint.

Wait, but `|>` should go through `pipeNodesRoot`, not `intersectNodesRoot`. Let me re-read `intersectOrPipeNodes`:

```js
let result = isPureIntersection ? _intersectNodes(l, r, ctx)
    : l.hasKindIn(...rootKinds) ?
        _pipeNodes(l, r, ctx)
        : _intersectNodes(l, r, ctx);
```

`isPureIntersection = !ctx.pipe || (!l.includesTransform && !r.includesTransform)`. Since `ctx.pipe = true`, `isPureIntersection = !true || (!false && !false) = false || true = true`. So it goes to `_intersectNodes` instead of `_pipeNodes`!

The condition `isPureIntersection` is incorrectly computed for this case. When `ctx.pipe` is true but neither operand has transforms, `isPureIntersection` is `true`, and it tries to intersect `string` and `number` which fails.

This is a bug or an intentional design where `|>` with non-morph operands is treated as intersection. But wait — the `|>` operator in ArkType's string syntax should create a morph. Let me check if the string parser handles this differently...

Actually, looking at the string parser flow more carefully: when parsing `"string |> number"`, the parser creates a morph node specifically. The `|>` operator in the string parser pushes the root to a branch and then continues. Let me trace through the `pushRootToBranch`:

ArkType

TypeScript's 1:1 validator, optimized from editor to runtime

ArkType is a runtime validation library that parses optimized validators from familiar, type-safe syntax. It can be used to check external data like JSON payloads or forms at the boundaries of your code (similar to Zod).

The `|>` operator in ArkType 2.2.0 might work differently. Let me try using the tuple expression syntax:

    at Disjoint.throw (/home/user/myproject/node_modules/@ark/schema/out/shared/disjoint.js:36:16)
    at Object.parseBranchTuple (/home/user/myproject/node_modules/arktype/out/parser/tupleExpressions.js:20:23)

part.text: Interesting — even `type('string', '|>', 'number')` fails. Let me check how the tuple expression parser handles `|>`:

I see. `"|>"` is handled by `parseBranchTuple` which calls `pipeNodesRoot(l, r, ctx.$)`. And `pipeNodesRoot` for two non-morph types falls through to `_intersectNodes`, which tries to intersect `string` and `number` — which fails.

The issue is that `pipeNodesRoot` treats `|>` as a pipe, but for non-morph operands, `isPureIntersection` becomes true and it tries to intersect them. This is a known behavior — `|>` between two types creates a morph where the first is the input and the second is the output. But the `pipeNodesRoot` function doesn't create a morph node properly for this case.

Wait, let me re-read `intersectOrPipeNodes`:

```js
let result = isPureIntersection ? _intersectNodes(l, r, ctx)
    : l.hasKindIn(...rootKinds) ?
        _pipeNodes(l, r, ctx)
        : _intersectNodes(l, r, ctx);
```

`isPureIntersection = !ctx.pipe || (!l.includesTransform && !r.includesTransform)`

For `pipeNodesRoot(string, number, $)`, `ctx.pipe = true`, `l.includesTransform = false`, `r.includesTransform = false`. So `isPureIntersection = !true || (!false && !false) = false || true = true`. This means it goes to `_intersectNodes` which fails.

This seems like a bug in ArkType 2.2.0, or perhaps `|>` between two non-morph types isn't supported. Let me try using the `.pipe()` method instead, which should work:

part.text: So `.pipe()` also fails with the same error. This is definitely a bug in ArkType 2.2.0 where piping a non-morph type to another type doesn't work. Let me check if there's a different way to do morphs. Let me try using the morph as a function:

output.output:
pipe with function expression: (In: string) => Out<unknown>
validate "42": 42
validate "hello": NaN

part.text:
OK so `.pipe()` with a function works. But the requirement says to use ArkType morphs with the `|>` / pipe operator — a "single declarative pipeline". The issue is that `type.string.pipe(type.number)` doesn't work in 2.2.0.

Let me check if there's a `string.numeric.parse` submodule that does exactly this:
